title: Using Data in Monetary Policymaking: A View from the Fed
summary: Framed as a methodological talk on how she uses data, the speech nonetheless delivers a clear policy read: the economy is in a good place close to both mandates, with wage growth no longer a significant source of inflation. Looking ahead she expects tariffs to push core PCE toward roughly 3% by year-end before resuming its decline, with some downward pressure on jobs and growth - upside inflation risk, downside activity risk. Given still-solid conditions she judges an actively patient approach to policy as appropriate, i.e., a data-dependent hold.

key_quotes.quote: Thus, in my view, an “actively patient” approach to monetary policy remains appropriate at this time.
it seems likely that core PCE inﬂation will be in the vicinity of 3 percent by year’s end, before resuming its decline.
key_quotes.context: Tariff-driven upside to inflation in the near term

key_quotes.quote: Broadly speaking, both wage measures suggest that the labor market is no longer a signiﬁcant source of price pressures (inﬂation).
key_quotes.context: Labor market not driving inflation; relatively relaxed on jobs

key_quotes.quote: the economy continues to be in a good place overall, close to the Congressionally mandated objectives of price stability and full employment.
